sections.summary: Combining sertraline with tramadol increases the risk of serotonin syndrome, since tramadol itself has serotonergic activity in addition to its opioid effect.
sections.mechanism: Sertraline blocks serotonin reuptake, and tramadol both inhibits serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake and, through its metabolites, weakly stimulates serotonin release. The combined serotonergic effect can produce excess synaptic serotonin.
sections.clinical_effects: Signs of serotonin syndrome may appear, including agitation, tremor, hyperreflexia, diaphoresis, tachycardia, and in severe cases hyperthermia and altered mental status; tramadol also independently lowers the seizure threshold, which sertraline may add to.
sections.management: Use the combination cautiously and only when necessary, starting at lower tramadol doses; avoid combining with other serotonergic agents, and discontinue promptly if serotonergic symptoms appear.
sections.monitoring: Educate patients on early symptoms of serotonin syndrome (agitation, sweating, tremor) and monitor closely after initiation or dose increases of either drug.

evidence.citation: Ultram (tramadol) FDA prescribing information, Warnings and Precautions: Serotonin Syndrome
